section.p: On this step you will be asked about how you plan to file your taxes.

fg-set.question: Do you know the filing status you will use when filing your taxes?
Which filing status will you use to file federal taxes in ?
fg-set.modal-link: Which filing status should I use?

fg-set.question: What was your marital status on December 31, ?
fg-set.modal-link: What does married and legally separated mean?

fg-set.question: When did your spouse pass away?
When did your spouse pass away?
When did your spouse pass away?
Are you married?
Do you intend to file a joint return with your deceased spouse for ?
Do you and your spouse intend to file a joint return for ?
Did your spouse live with you in your home at any time during the last six months of (including any temporary absences)?
Did you and your spouse have the same principal residence for the last 6 months of ?
Were you legally separated according to your state law under a written separation agreement, or a decree of separate maintenance and you did not live with your spouse on the last day of ?
Were you entitled to file a joint return with your spouse for the year your spouse died?
In , did you pay more than half the cost of keeping up a home in which you lived that was also the main home of your child or stepchild for all of (including any temporary absences)?
fg-set.modal-link: How do I calculate "Keeping Up a Home"?

fg-set.question: Can you claim your child or stepchild who lived with you as a dependent for ?

Could you claim your child or stepchild as a dependent EXCEPT that, for : 1) The child had gross income that was above the threshold of for ; 2) The child filed a joint return; or 3) You could be claimed as a dependent on someone else's return?
Did you pay more than 50% of the cost of keeping up a home in ?
fg-set.modal-link: How do I calculate "Keeping Up a Home"?

input.option: Where you lived more than half the year with a dependent (such as a child, grandchild, sibling, niece, nephew, parent or grandparent)
Where you lived for more than half the year with a child who isn't your dependent even though you're the custodial parent (because of the rule for children of divorced or separated parents)
For a parent you can claim as a dependent, but who didn't live with you

fg-set.question: Does your spouse have a valid Social Security number that was issued on or before the due date of your tax return (including extensions)?
Did your spouse have a valid Social Security number on the day they passed away?
Was your spouse alive on December 31, ?
Please select the range that describes your spouse's age when they diedat the end of
Do you plan to claim children or other relatives with valid Social Security numbers for the EITC?
fg-set.modal-link: Who can I claim as a dependent?

fg-set.question: Was youryour and your spouse's main home in the U.S. for more than half of ?
fg-set.modal-link: What counts as a main home in the U.S.?

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: You don't qualify for EITC without a qualifying child unless you, or your spouse if filing a joint return, are between age 25 and 64.
For more information, see Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: You can claim the EIC if you are married, not filing a joint return, had a qualifying child who lived with you for more than half of 2025, and either of the following apply:

ul.li: You lived apart from your spouse for the last 6 months of 2025, or

You are legally separated according to your state law under a written separation agreement, or a decree of separate maintenance and you didn't live in the same household as your spouse at the end of 2025.

fg-alert.p: For more information see About Publication 501, Dependents, Standard Deduction, and Filing Information. (opens in a new tab)

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: You can't claim the EITC because your spouse doesn't have a valid Social Security number issued on or before the due date of your tax return.

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: You can't claim the EITC if you choose to file a joint return with your deceased spouse because your spouse did not have a valid social security number on the day they passed away.
You may qualify for the EITC if you don't file a joint return with your deceased spouse.

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: To claim the EITC, your home must have been in the United States for more than half the year. The United States includes the 50 states and the District of Columbia.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Which filing status should I use?

modal-content.p: If there's no change in your marital status or family situation this year, then you may want to use the same filing status from your tax return.
If you had a change in your marital status or household in the past year, you may need to use a different filing status for a more accurate estimate.
Each filing status has different requirements.

ul.li: You're married or your spouse died during the year
You don't plan to file together with your spouse (most couples save money by filing jointly)
You can't be married (or you're considered unmarried for tax purposes)
You paid more than half the cost of keeping up a home for a family member or member of your household for a specific amount of time, and they must meet certain requirements
You have a dependent child who lives with you all year
You pay more than half the cost of keeping up your home

p.a: Learn more about filing statuses (opens in a new tab).

modal-content.p: To claim EITC with a qualifying child your child must have a valid SSN issued on or before the due date of your return (including extensions).
If the Social Security card reads "Valid for work with DHS authorization" or “Valid for work with INS authorization” on it, it is a valid SSN if the authorization is still valid.
For the EITC, we don't accept:

ul.li: Social Security number issued solely to allow the recipient of the SSN to apply for or receive a federally funded benefit such as Medicaid

modal-content.p: Your child is a qualifying child if your child meets four tests. The four tests are:
Review IRS Publication 596 for more information on a valid Social Security Number and if you have a qualifying child.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Main Home in the United States

modal-content.p: Your home must have been in the United States for more than half of .
The United States includes the 50 states and the District of Columbia. It does not include United States possessions such as Guam, the Virgin Islands, or Puerto Rico.
modal-content.h3: Military Personnel Stationed Outside the United States
modal-content.p: If you're a member of the United States military and you were stationed outside the United States on extended active duty, select Yes.
If the total amount you paid is more than half the total cost, you meet the requirement of paying more than half the cost of keeping up the home.

ul.li: Rent, mortgage interest, real estate taxes, and home insurance

modal-content.p: Note: Costs paid with public assistance are considered costs paid by the taxpayer.

ul.li: Medical treatment, medical insurance payments, and prescription drugs
Transportation costs like insurance, lease payments, or public transportation
Value of your services or those of a member of your household

modal-content.p: "Legally separated" for this purpose means legally separated under a decree of divorce or separate maintenance.
Some states allow common law marriages. Check with your state to see if you are considered married under common law.

You can choose to file one return with your spouse by selecting Married as your filing status. This allows you to combine your income and expenses when figuring your credits, deductions, and tax liability.

If your spouse died during , you can choose to file one return with your spouse by selecting Married Filing Jointly as your filing status. This allows you to combine your income and expenses when figuring your credits, deductions, and tax liability.
A person is considered to have lived with you during periods of time when one or both of you are temporarily absent due to special circumstances such as:
It must be reasonable to assume that the absent person will return to the home after the temporary absence.
You must have a valid Social Security number (SSN) that is issued by the Social Security Administration on or before the due date of your tax return (including extensions).
If your Social Security card reads “Valid for work with DHS authorization” or “Valid for work with INS authorization” on it, it is a valid SSN if the authorization is still valid.
For the EITC, we don't accept:

ul.li: Social Security number issued after the due date of the return, including extensions
Social Security number issued solely to apply for or receive a federally funded benefit such as Medicaid

modal-content.p: See Publication 596 for more information on a valid Social Security Number.
Changing from Married Filing Jointly to a different filing status will clear your previously entered qualifying child data. Do you still wish to continue?

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can I claim as a dependent?

modal-content.p: Dependents are typically:

ul.li: Students under the age of 24 who live with their parents
Relatives or members of your household who get more than half of their financial support from you and who have a gross income under

modal-content.p: You can’t claim your spouse as a dependent if you file jointly.
